name: Austin Independent School District
districtFact: Austin ISD was founded in 1839 — one of the oldest public school districts in Texas, established while Texas was still an independent republic. Its flagship magnet, LASA (Liberal Arts and Science Academy), is consistently ranked among the top public high schools in the state and requires a competitive district application.

about.title: About Austin Independent School District
about.content: Many families relocating to Austin discover quickly that living in 'Austin' doesn't automatically mean their children attend Austin Independent School District. Austin ISD covers the city proper, but the greater metro area includes more than a dozen separate independent school districts — Round Rock ISD, Pflugerville ISD, Leander ISD, Lake Travis ISD, Manor ISD, and Eanes ISD each operate entirely independently, with their own academic calendars, school choice programs, and attendance boundaries. Your assigned district is determined by your street address, not by the city name or ZIP code on your lease. This is typically the first thing families new to the metro area need to sort out. Austin ISD itself serves approximately 73,000 students across more than 130 schools in the city of Austin — Texas's state capital — and has been doing so since 1839, when it was established during the Republic of Texas era, making it one of the oldest public school districts in the state. The district is economically and racially diverse: roughly 60% of students qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, and the student population reflects Austin's growth as a tech hub, with large Latino and African-American communities alongside growing numbers of families arriving from California, New York, and Washington state through tech-sector relocation. Austin ISD operates on a traditional two-semester, 180-instructional-day calendar, as required by the Texas Education Agency.

about.title: Austin's School Year and the City Calendar
about.content: One thing that catches many families off guard: Spring Break in Austin ISD almost always lands during South by Southwest (SXSW), one of the world's largest tech, music, and film festivals. The practical result is that Austin-Bergstrom International Airport (AUS) and downtown hotels price like peak season the same week families want to leave for Spring Break. Families who book flights in February rather than March typically pay significantly less. It's worth looking up the specific overlap — SXSW dates shift slightly year to year, and some years Austin ISD's break aligns perfectly with the festival's core weekend while other years it runs adjacent. Austin City Limits Music Festival (ACL) runs in early October; it's not a school holiday, but Saturday attendance is popular with older students and families. The Formula One United States Grand Prix at Circuit of the Americas (COTA), typically in late October, brings noticeable traffic to South Austin on race weekend. The tech sector also shapes enrollment patterns in a way most families don't anticipate: Austin ISD sees a consistent wave of mid-year transfers, particularly in January, from families relocating from Apple, Tesla, Oracle, and Dell campuses who didn't time their move to the August school start. Kindergarten cohorts at campuses near the Domain and North Austin tech corridor can include a notable proportion of children who just moved from California or Washington.

about.title: School Choice and Getting Enrolled
about.content: Parents who have done their homework on Austin ISD often already know about LASA — the Liberal Arts and Science Academy — which is consistently ranked among the top 10 public high schools in Texas. What surprises them is how early the application process starts: LASA applications typically close months before the school year begins, and families who call in January asking to apply for the following September have usually missed the window. The same is true for the Dual Language (Spanish-English) program, one of the most established bilingual education programs in Central Texas; applications open each January for fall placement, and popular campuses fill via lottery. Early College High School programs, run through Austin Community College, let students earn transferable college credit at no cost while still enrolled — another Austin ISD option that draws families specifically to the district over neighboring suburban alternatives. For new enrollees, four steps determine how smoothly the process goes: use the AISD school finder at austinisd.org to confirm your assigned campus by street address (not neighborhood, since boundaries update annually); verify that a child turning 5 meets Texas's September 1 kindergarten cutoff; gather Texas-specific immunization documentation, which may differ from another state's requirements; and if a student has an existing IEP or 504 plan, bring documentation — Austin ISD is required to honor accommodations promptly on enrollment. Specialty program applications (LASA, Dual Language) have earlier deadlines than general enrollment — October is the right time to start, not after New Year.

content: Spring Break overlaps with SXSW (March 15–19): downtown Austin is extremely crowded. Book out-of-town travel early if you plan to leave the city, as Austin-Bergstrom airport and rental car agencies are at peak demand.
Individual campuses schedule additional PLED Days not shown on the main district calendar. Get your school's complete no-school day list directly from the campus office in the first week of August.

Austin ISD attendance boundaries are revised regularly due to rapid population growth. Confirm your assigned school with the district office before enrollment, even if a neighbor's child attends a nearby school.

Back-to-school shopping is best done by early August — Austin's August heat regularly exceeds 100°F, and popular uniform and supply stores near schools fill up the week before school starts (around August 11–14).

districtFaqs.q: Is Austin ISD the same as the University of Texas at Austin (UT Austin)?
districtFaqs.a: No. Austin Independent School District (Austin ISD) is the K-12 public school system serving the city of Austin. The University of Texas at Austin (UT Austin, often called 'UT') is a separate public research university with its own semester academic calendar. UT Austin's schedule — including its start dates, finals, and graduation — has no effect on Austin ISD school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Does Austin ISD spring break overlap with SXSW?
districtFaqs.a: Yes, in most years Austin ISD's Spring Break falls during or immediately adjacent to South by Southwest (SXSW), which typically runs in mid-March. In 2026-2027, Austin ISD Spring Break is March 15–19 — overlapping with or falling adjacent to SXSW's mid-March event window. Families planning local activities during this period should expect heavily increased hotel prices, restaurant wait times, and traffic congestion throughout central Austin.

districtFaqs.q: What are PLED Days in Austin ISD?
districtFaqs.a: PLED Days (Professional Learning and Engagement Days) are district-designated no-school days used for teacher professional development. Austin ISD schedules system-wide PLED Days (such as September 21 and April 30) that apply to all campuses. In addition, individual schools may schedule their own site-level PLED Days, which are separate from the district-wide calendar. Check your child's school directly for the full list of campus-specific no-school days each year.

districtFaqs.q: How do I find my assigned school in Austin ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Austin ISD uses a geographic attendance zone system. To find your assigned school, use the district's official school finder tool at austinisd.org with your home address. Do not rely on a neighbor's assignment — Austin's rapid growth means attendance boundaries are updated regularly, and families on the same block may be assigned to different campuses from year to year.

districtFaqs.q: Does Austin ISD follow the same calendar as Round Rock ISD or Leander ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Not necessarily. Round Rock ISD, Leander ISD, Pflugerville ISD, and other districts in the Austin metro area each set their own academic calendars. Spring break dates, the first day of school, and early-release days may differ by a week or more between Austin ISD and neighboring suburban districts. Families with children in multiple districts — or coordinating with grandparents in Round Rock or Leander — should verify each district's official calendar separately before booking travel or childcare.

districtFaqs.q: When is STAAR testing in Austin ISD, and does Austin ISD have school on Veterans Day?
districtFaqs.a: STAAR (State of Texas Assessments of Academic Readiness) is Texas's standardized testing program administered in grades 3–12. In Austin ISD, STAAR testing windows typically run in March and May, with specific dates varying by grade and subject each year. Austin ISD posts the current testing calendar on its website once the Texas Education Agency releases the official window. Families should avoid scheduling travel or appointments on STAAR dates; check the AISD academic calendar each fall for the confirmed window. Regarding Veterans Day: Austin ISD observes November 11 as an official school holiday — students do not have school on Veterans Day. Whether it falls on a weekday or creates a longer break depends on the calendar for that specific year; check the year-specific calendar for adjacent employee planning days.

districtFaqs.q: Does Austin ISD close for snow days or winter weather?
districtFaqs.a: Yes, but infrequently — Austin averages far less winter precipitation than North Texas, and true winter weather events are uncommon most years. When they do occur, however, closures can span multiple days. The February 2021 winter storm (Winter Storm Uri) forced Austin ISD to close for over a week. Texas law requires districts to make up lost instructional days; in practice, Austin ISD builds buffer days into the calendar and may extend the school year in severe winters. Austin does not operate on a routine 'snow day' schedule — when closures happen, they are treated as emergency events rather than expected seasonal occurrences. Austin ISD announces closures and delays via the district website, ParentSquare notifications, and local TV stations (KVUE, KXAN, FOX 7 Austin).

districtFaqs.q: When are Austin ISD report cards and how many grading periods are there?
districtFaqs.a: Austin ISD divides the school year into six grading periods, each approximately six weeks long. Report cards are typically issued within a week of each grading period ending. Progress reports — informal mid-period check-ins — are issued roughly midway through each grading period. Parents can view grades and attendance in real time through the Home Access Center (HAC) portal at austinisd.org. Parents new to Texas schools should note that Austin ISD uses a numerical grade scale (0–100) rather than letter grades for most grade levels. Setting up a HAC account during the first week of school is strongly recommended, as it also provides early-warning visibility on attendance patterns before a formal report card is issued.

districtFaqs.q: Does Austin ISD offer Pre-K, and how does Pre-K enrollment work?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Austin ISD offers Pre-Kindergarten for 4-year-olds who meet eligibility criteria set by the Texas Education Agency. Priority is given to children who are English Language Learners, qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, are in foster care or homeless, are children of active-duty military, or meet other TEA eligibility categories. Austin ISD Pre-K is a full-day program available at select campuses — not every elementary school offers it. A child must turn 4 on or before September 1 to enroll. Pre-K registration typically opens in spring for the following school year; contact your assigned elementary school or the AISD Pre-K office for current registration dates and to confirm eligibility. Families who do not meet TEA eligibility criteria should ask about fee-based Pre-K options, which some campuses offer when space allows.

districtFaqs.q: When is Austin ISD graduation, and where are ceremonies held?
districtFaqs.a: Austin ISD high school graduation ceremonies are typically held in late May or early June, following the last week of instruction. Each high school holds its own separate ceremony — dates and venues are announced by spring each year. Families should note that Austin ISD graduation weekend often coincides with end-of-year events at UT Austin: downtown Austin, hotels near campus, and I-35 can be congested simultaneously. Most Austin ISD high schools post graduation dates and venue information by February; check your specific high school's website rather than relying on a district-wide announcement.

districtFaqs.q: When does Austin ISD open enrollment open, and what documents are needed to register?
districtFaqs.a: Austin ISD's general enrollment window for new students typically opens in late winter or early spring for the following school year. Kindergarten registration is announced in spring, with the specific date posted on the AISD website and typically promoted through local media. For mid-year transfers from another school or district, Austin ISD accepts enrollment on a rolling basis — contact the Enrollment Center or your assigned campus directly. Required documents include proof of Austin ISD address (lease, utility bill, or mortgage statement), the student's birth certificate, Texas-required immunization records, and prior school records or transcripts if transferring. Specialty programs — LASA, Dual Language, Early College — have separate application processes with earlier deadlines, typically October through January for the following fall. If a child has an existing IEP or 504 plan, bring documentation — Austin ISD is required to honor existing accommodations promptly upon enrollment.

districtFaqs.q: What time does school start and end in Austin ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Bell schedules in Austin ISD vary by campus and school level — Austin ISD does not use a single district-wide start time. Elementary schools typically begin between 7:30 and 8:00 a.m. and dismiss in the early to mid-afternoon. Middle and high schools may start as early as 7:20 a.m. at some campuses. Check your specific campus's bell schedule on the school's website or contact the school directly. Families new to Austin should build in extra time during the school year: I-35 and MoPac (Loop 1) are significantly more congested during school months than in summer, and carpool pickup/dropoff at many campuses can add 15–25 minutes to departure times compared to the first few days of school before routines settle.

livingHere.intro: Austin has transformed rapidly over the past decade into a major tech hub, drawing campuses from Apple, Tesla, Dell, Oracle, and dozens of other companies — while also remaining the home of SXSW, UT Austin, and a live music culture that shapes the city's identity. For families relocating to the area, a few Austin-specific realities directly intersect with the school calendar.

highlights.label: SXSW Overlaps Spring Break
highlights.detail: South by Southwest — a major tech, music, and film festival — runs in mid-March and almost always overlaps with Austin ISD's Spring Break. Downtown hotels surge in price, rental cars sell out, and airport demand peaks. Families planning out-of-town travel should book months in advance.

highlights.label: Austin-Bergstrom Airport (AUS)
highlights.detail: AUS has expanded significantly but still faces congestion during SXSW, UT graduation weekends, and the week school lets out in late May. Spring Break departures during SXSW week require booking at least 8–10 weeks out for reasonable fares and rental car availability.

highlights.label: Tech Sector Employment Cycles
highlights.detail: Apple, Tesla, Dell, and Oracle all have major Austin campuses, driving consistent inbound relocation. Austin ISD sees a high volume of mid-year transfers from California, Washington, and New York — families arriving via tech relocations often enroll in January or at semester break.

highlights.label: Rapid Population Growth & Traffic
highlights.detail: Austin's population has grown by over 20% in a decade, and I-35 and MoPac (Loop 1) corridors rank among the most congested in Texas. School-year morning commutes can add 20–40 minutes compared to summer — families new to Austin should test their school-day commute route before the first week.

highlights.label: Barton Springs & Summer Planning
highlights.detail: Barton Springs Pool (a natural spring-fed pool in Zilker Park) is the most popular post-school-year destination in Austin. It closes periodically for water quality and drought conditions — check Austin Parks before planning end-of-school celebrations or summer camp visits. Summer camp programs tied to the pool and Zilker Park fill in late March; register immediately after spring break.

highlights.label: Extreme Summer Heat
highlights.detail: Austin regularly records 100°F+ days from mid-July through early September, covering the entire back-to-school period. UIL heat protocols limit outdoor athletic practices; families should expect restricted outdoor recess and after-school sports activity during the first two weeks of school.

highlights.label: UT Football Game Days
highlights.detail: University of Texas home football games draw 100,000+ fans to Darrell K Royal Stadium, turning central Austin into gridlock on fall Saturdays. Families with after-school activities, Saturday sports, or evening events near UT, I-35, or MoPac should check the Longhorns home schedule before booking. Late-afternoon game days can affect school-adjacent neighborhoods for several hours.

highlights.label: Texas State Capitol and Field Trips
highlights.detail: The Texas State Capitol is a major destination for Austin ISD field trips, particularly in fall and spring semesters. When the Texas Legislature is in session (January–June of odd-numbered years), downtown parking and traffic near Congress Avenue increase significantly on school days — a practical consideration for families driving downtown during session months.

highlights.label: Austin ISD Signature Programs
highlights.detail: Austin ISD operates several distinctive district programs not available in most surrounding suburban districts. The Dual Language program (Spanish-English) begins at elementary level and is one of the most established bilingual programs in Central Texas — applications open each January for fall enrollment. LASA (Liberal Arts and Science Academy) is Austin ISD's flagship magnet high school and is consistently ranked among the top public high schools in Texas, drawing academically competitive students from across the district. Early College High School programs allow students to earn college credit through Austin Community College while still enrolled in AISD. Families interested in any of these programs should contact the district's school choice office by October — competitive programs fill early and waitlists form quickly.

compareIntro: Austin ISD covers the city of Austin proper and is surrounded by a ring of independently operated suburban districts — Round Rock, Pflugerville, Leander, Lake Travis, Manor, and Eanes ISDs each set their own academic calendars. The most practically relevant comparison for most Austin families is against these nearby suburban districts, where spring break timing, first-day dates, and early-release schedules often differ by a week or more. Austin ISD's calendar also reflects Austin-specific factors: PLED Days scheduled around district professional development needs, and spring break timing that consistently overlaps with SXSW. Compared to other large Texas ISDs — Houston, Dallas, San Antonio's Northside — Austin ISD's calendar is similar in structure but set independently; spring break weeks and holiday patterns rarely align precisely even within the same state.

relatedDistricts.name: Houston Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Texas's largest district (190,000+ students), set independently from Austin ISD. Houston and Austin school years typically start within a few days of each other, but spring break and early-release days frequently fall in different weeks. Families comparing the two most common tech-relocation destinations in Texas — Austin and Houston — should verify each district's spring break separately before booking travel.

relatedDistricts.name: Dallas Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: North Texas's largest urban district (140,000+ students), operating on a similar TEA-required 180-day structure but set independently of Austin ISD. Dallas ISD occasionally starts the school year a week earlier than Austin ISD, and the two districts rarely align on spring break. Families comparing Austin and Dallas for relocation should check each district's calendar each year rather than assuming they match.

relatedDistricts.name: Northside Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: San Antonio's largest district (100,000+ students), located roughly 80 miles south of Austin. Northside ISD sets its calendar independently of Austin ISD; spring break dates sometimes align and sometimes differ by a week. Families coordinating travel or childcare across Austin and San Antonio should verify both district calendars each year.

relatedDistricts.name: Round Rock Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Round Rock ISD is Austin ISD's most directly adjacent suburban neighbor to the north, serving Round Rock, Cedar Park, and portions of north Austin along the US-183 and I-35 corridors. The two districts set calendars independently — spring break dates, first-day timing, and no-school days (including Austin ISD's culturally inclusive PLED Days) frequently differ. Families in north Austin near the district boundary should verify address assignments at both districts, as the line runs through some neighborhoods; Round Rock ISD does not observe Diwali, Dolores Huerta Day, or Eid al-Fitr as school closures.
